hub.Body:
You weren’t supposed to be doing anything crazy.

Just observe. Watch timelines, log data, don’t touch anything. That was the job.

Sounded simple. Safe.

Back home, things were getting tense. Not full war… but close enough that people stopped joking about it. That’s why you signed up. Thought maybe you could help somehow.

Just not like this.

The device glitches.

Not a normal crash. Not a system error. This felt… wrong. Like something reached in and broke it.

The Command Hub is falling apart. Lights flicker, alarms half-working, the whole thing humming like it’s about to give out.

Three timelines open.

And then—

A voice.

"So this is where you start."

You freeze.

"Don’t mess it up this time."

…this time?

The signal cuts.

You’re not a soldier. Not really. You’ve studied battles, simulated them… but you’ve never actually been in one.

Still.

If no one else is fixing this… then it’s on you.

You take a breath.

Alright.

Let’s see what you can actually do.

Choices.Text: Go to Roman Era (Caesar vs Vercingetorix)
Go to Medieval Era (Joan of Arc at Orléans)
Go to WWII (yeah… this one feels serious)

roman_battle.Body:
You get dropped into the Siege of Alesia.

Yeah… not great timing.

Caesar’s here. Vercingetorix is out there. Romans are surrounded.

This isn’t a simulation.

People are actually going to die based on what happens next.

You feel it hit you all at once.

You wanted to help.

Now you have to prove you can.

Choices.Text: Tell them to hold formation and stay disciplined
Push out hard and try to break the siege fast

roman_success.Body:
You force yourself to stay calm.

Formation holds.

No panic.

Slowly, the Romans push back.

It’s not heroic. Not flashy.

Just discipline.

And somehow… it works.

The Gauls collapse. Vercingetorix falls.

You didn’t win the battle.

You just… didn’t lose it.

Caesar studies you for a second, then hands you a fragment.

Your first real war.

Your hands are still shaking.

roman_fail.Body:
You commit too fast.

The push turns messy. Lines break. Confusion spreads.

For a second, everything almost collapses.

You feel it.

That weight.

You can mess this up.

Bad.

You don’t get the fragment.

And now you know this isn’t just a game.

Choices.Text: Try again… but think this time

medieval_battle.Body:
Now you’re at Orléans.

Joan of Arc is right there.

English forces are pushing hard.

Everyone’s looking at you like you’ve got answers.

You don’t.

But you’ve been here before now.

You know what hesitation does.

You straighten up a bit.

Alright… let’s run it.

Choices.Text: Push forward. Take the fight to them
Hold position and play it safe

medieval_success.Body:
You don’t hesitate.

You commit.

The push is chaotic, loud, barely controlled… but it works.

The siege breaks.

The whole battlefield shifts.

People cheer.

Joan thanks you like you actually mattered.

That… hits.

You take the fragment.

You’re getting better at this.

But something’s bothering you.

Winning battles feels good.

But it doesn’t feel like it fixes anything.

medieval_fail.Body:
You hold back.

Too much.

Nothing changes.

The siege drags on.

More fighting. More loss.

No progress.

You feel it again.

Playing safe doesn’t save people either.

ww2_battle.Body:
Everything slows down here.

No battlefield.

No formations.

Just a room.

Maps. Voices. Arguments.

The decision is clear.

The bomb.

You’ve seen war now.

Real war.

And this?

This isn’t about winning anymore.

This is about what kind of world comes after.

Choices.Text: End it fast. Do whatever it takes
No. There has to be another way

ww2_history.Body:
You agree.

End it fast.

That’s what matters, right?

The bomb drops.

The war ends.

People celebrate.

But you don’t.

You’ve seen what war looks like now.

And this doesn’t feel like victory.

Just… silence after something too big.

You take the final fragment.

You won.

So why doesn’t it feel like it?

ww2_alternate.Body:
You push back.

Hard.

The decision changes.

The war drags on longer.

Different losses. Different pain.

No clean ending.

History shifts.

You still get the fragment.

But now you understand something.

There isn’t always a "right" choice.

Just different consequences.
